qa.overall_assessment: All three bugs are real, confirmed by live Python execution against the actual source file. Line number references are accurate. Acceptance criteria are testable and sufficient. Scope is correctly bounded. Risk level 'high' is appropriate. One spec_text_vs_execution_drift objection is raised against the BUG 3 Technical Hints implementation example, which gives wrong guidance. The objection requires BA to correct the hint before dev dispatch. A secondary minor finding about the regex fast-path (Codex finding 5) is also raised. All other Codex findings are classified out-of-scope relative to the stated user requirement.

bugs_verified.claim: text.strip() == "-c" never matches combined flag forms like -lc, -ce, -xc, +c
bugs_verified.evidence: grep confirmed line 298 contains exact match. Live execution: strip_non_executable_contexts("bash -lc 'rm /tmp/x'") returns "bash -lc 'rm /tmp/x'" — rm hidden inside quoted token. Same for -ce and +c forms. grep -qE '(^|[ \t;|&(])rm\s' returns False on all combined-flag outputs.

bugs_verified.claim: _find_command_word advances one token past sudo, then returns the next word (-u) as the command word
bugs_verified.evidence: Traced _find_command_word tokens for 'sudo -u root bash -c': [sudo, space, -u, space, root, space, bash, space, -c]. cmd_idx=2 pointing at '-u', not 'bash'. _basename('-u') not in _SHELL_INTERPS. strip_non_executable_contexts("sudo -u root bash -c 'rm /tmp/x'") returns 'sudo -u root bash -c ' — payload stripped. Same result for sudo -H and sudo --.

bugs_verified.claim: regex r"\|\s*(bash|sh|zsh|dash)\b" does not match | /bin/bash or | /usr/bin/bash
bugs_verified.evidence: Traced heredoc parsing for 'cat <<\'EOF\' | /bin/bash': after = ' | /bin/bash'. re.search(r"\|\s*(bash|sh|zsh|dash)\b", after) returns None. strip_non_executable_contexts returns heredoc body as empty line — rm not visible.

success_criteria_results.source_phrase: Combined flags: bash -lc / bash -ec where -lc contains -c but does not equal -c
success_criteria_results.criterion: strip_non_executable_contexts('bash -lc \'rm /tmp/x\'') exposes rm as executable text
success_criteria_results.verification_method: Live execution of strip_non_executable_contexts with combined-flag inputs; grep pattern match on output
success_criteria_results.details: rm NOT visible in current output. Bug is real. ACs 1, 2, 10 cover this case. Fix approach (c in text.strip()[1:]) is correct.

success_criteria_results.source_phrase: sudo -u root bash -c '...' — the -u root shifts the position so bash is not recognized
success_criteria_results.details: cmd_idx=2 points at -u (not bash). rm NOT visible. ACs 3-5 cover sudo variants. Fix approach (_SUDO_OPTS_WITH_ARG table) is correct.
success_criteria_results.evidence: strip_non_executable_contexts("sudo -u root bash -c 'rm /tmp/x'") = 'sudo -u root bash -c ' — payload stripped

success_criteria_results.source_phrase: | bash does NOT match absolute paths like | /bin/bash or | /usr/bin/bash
success_criteria_results.criterion: heredoc body in 'cat <<\'EOF\' | /bin/bash\nrm /tmp/x\nEOF' is preserved as executable text
success_criteria_results.verification_method: Live execution; regex trace against after variable
success_criteria_results.details: after = ' | /bin/bash'; regex returns None. Body zeroed out. ACs 6-8 cover this case. Fix approach needs correction (see issues).
success_criteria_results.evidence: strip_non_executable_contexts returns heredoc body as empty line for /bin/bash and /usr/bin/bash

success_criteria_results.criterion: All existing tests in hooks/tests/test_bash_safety_context.py continue to pass
success_criteria_results.details: 30 tests collected, 30 passed in 0.03s. Baseline confirms all existing tests pass before any fix is applied.

risk_level_verification.rationale: The file runs on every Bash tool call as a PreToolUse hook. A false negative (missed danger token) silently reduces safety coverage. A regression that widens false positives can block legitimate operations. Both failure modes are high-impact on a production safety system.

issues.finding: The Technical Hints say: 'Preferred pattern: after the existing regex fast-path, also check whether _first_word(after) is in _SHELL_INTERPS' and 'Note that after is the text after the heredoc <<EOF marker on the same line, which may contain | /bin/bash. _first_word() tokenises and returns the first command word with its basename resolved — exactly what is needed.' This is factually wrong. For 'cat <<\'EOF\' | /bin/bash', after = ' | /bin/bash'. _tokenize_segment treats '|' as a 'word' token (it is not in the quoted/space/hash/dollar branches and the continuation loop stops at the next space). _find_command_word returns idx=1 pointing at '|'. Therefore _first_word(' | /bin/bash') = '|', NOT 'bash'. A dev following the hint literally would call _first_word(after) and get '|', which is not in _SHELL_INTERPS, and the fix would silently fail AC6 and AC7.
issues.evidence: Live execution: _first_word(' | /bin/bash') = '|'. _first_word('| /bin/bash') = '|'. The pipe character is tokenized as ('|', 'word') and returned as the first command word. _first_word(' /bin/bash') = 'bash' (works only when pipe is absent).
issues.correct_approach: M3's requirement text correctly says 'calling _first_word() on the segment after |' (the pipe target segment, not the entire after string). The Technical Hints example must be corrected to: split after on '|', skip the part before the first pipe, then apply _first_word to each subsequent segment. Codex proposes: any(_first_word(seg) in _SHELL_INTERPS for seg in after.split('|')[1:]). This correctly handles both single-pipe ('| /bin/bash') and multi-pipe ('| tee /tmp/log | /usr/bin/bash') cases.
issues.proposed_fix: Replace the Technical Hints BUG 3 implementation example from '_first_word(after)' to: 'split after on "|", skip the segment before the first pipe, check any(_first_word(seg) in _SHELL_INTERPS for seg in after.split("|")[1:])'. Update the note to explain that after includes the pipe character itself, so direct _first_word(after) returns "|" not the shell name.
issues.ac_impact: ACs 6-8 are correctly stated and testable. The correctness of the ACs is not in question. Only the Technical Hints implementation example is wrong. A dev who reads M3's requirement text carefully ('segment after |') can still implement correctly. But the Hints create a trap.

issues.finding: The Technical Hints say to keep the existing regex r"\|\s*(bash|sh|zsh|dash)\b" as a fast-path. However, \b in regex matches the word boundary before '.', so '| bash.old' IS matched by this regex (bash.old contains 'bash' followed by non-word '.', which creates a \b). AC8 tests '/tmp/bash.old' (absolute path with directory), which is correctly NOT matched by the regex (because '/tmp/bash.old' does not start with the pipe-to-bare-name pattern). But '| bash.old' (bare name with extension) WOULD be matched by the regex as a false positive. If the regex is kept as a fast-path, this false positive is preserved. The correct approach (M3's intent) is to replace the regex with the parsed basename approach, which correctly handles all cases.
issues.evidence: re.search(r'\|\s*(bash|sh|zsh|dash)\b', '| bash.old') is truthy. re.search same pattern for '| bash.old.2' is also truthy. The \b matches between 'h' and '.'.
issues.proposed_fix: Update Technical Hints to recommend replacing the regex entirely with the parsed pipe-segment approach rather than keeping it as a fast-path. This avoids the bash.old false-positive edge case.
issues.ac_impact: AC8 tests /tmp/bash.old and passes both before and after fix (absolute path form does not match the old regex). A bare 'bash.old' case is not covered by AC8 but would be a false positive if regex is kept.

out_of_scope_observations.observation: Codex finding 1: doas -u root bash -c and run0 --user root bash -c produce the same bypass as sudo -u root bash -c (confirmed by live execution). doas and run0 are privilege-escalating wrappers semantically identical to sudo but are in _WRAPPER_PREFIXES without option-arg skipping. BA's out_of_scope_observations lists env/timeout/nice/ionice but not doas/run0. Both are security-relevant wrappers. They fall in the same C1 deferred class but should be documented in out_of_scope_observations.

Codex finding 2: sudo arity table is incomplete for sudo -p (prompt), -h (host), -T (timestamp timeout), -A (askpass). These also take arguments and produce the same bypass pattern. This is the same class as the env/timeout/nice/ionice C1 deferrals.

codex_consult.feedback_summary: Codex raised 5 findings: (1) BLOCKING — BUG2 under-scoped (env/timeout/nice/ionice/doas/run0 wrappers have same bypass); (2) MAJOR — sudo arity table likely incomplete (missing -p/-h/-T/-A); (3) MAJOR — BUG3 advisory correct but multi-pipe case needs handling; (4) MAJOR — ACs are library-level only, no end-to-end hook tests; (5) MINOR — regex fast-path preserves '| bash.old' false positive.
codex_consult.feedback_incorporated: Finding 1 classified out-of-scope per user requirement's three-bug framing; doas/run0 added to out_of_scope_observations as security_relevant. Finding 2 classified out-of-scope (same C1 class). Finding 3 incorporated: QA advisory for BUG 3 Technical Hints updated to use any(...for seg in after.split('|')[1:]) approach and add multi-pipe coverage note. Finding 4 classified out-of-scope (library-level tests are correct scope per requirement). Finding 5 incorporated as minor I2 issue about regex fast-path false positive.

qa.approval_notes: The BA spec is approved for dev dispatch with two required corrections: (1) The Technical Hints for BUG 3 must be updated to replace '_first_word(after)' with the pipe-splitting approach — '_first_word(after)' returns '|' not 'bash', which would silently fail AC6 and AC7 if followed literally. The corrected hint should use any(_first_word(seg) in _SHELL_INTERPS for seg in after.split('|')[1:]). (2) The Technical Hints should recommend replacing the existing regex rather than keeping it as a fast-path, to avoid the '| bash.old' false-positive edge case. Neither issue affects the correctness of the ACs or the bug existence — both are Technical Hints inaccuracies. M3's requirement text ('segment after |') is correctly stated. Dev who reads M3 carefully can implement correctly even without the corrected hint, but the Hints as written create a trap that would produce a silently wrong implementation passing no AC tests. These corrections are required before dev dispatch.
